Final Project Task 1 - Data Collection and Overfitting¶

Step 1: Import the required Python libraries¶

In [ ]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow import keras
from tensorflow.keras import layers, regularizers
from tensorflow.keras.layers import BatchNormalization, Dropout, Conv1D, MaxPooling1D, Flatten, SimpleRNN, GRU, Bidirectional
from tensorflow.keras.metrics import RootMeanSquaredError
from tensorflow.keras.callbacks import ModelCheckpoint, EarlyStopping
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler                      # For normalizing the data.
from scipy.interpolate import interp1d                              # For applying Time Warping augmentation technique to a model.

Step 2: Collect the time-series data for Bitcoin¶

2.1: Upload the Bitcoin dataset saved on Google Drive¶

The series of models that will be created will rely on a dataset called BTC_USD.csv. The dataset contains several different features (columns) that include day-to-day data over the course of 5 years (5/3/2019 to 5/3/2024):

1.) Open Price

2.) High Price

3.) Low Price

4.) Close Price

5.) Adjusted Close Price

6.) Volume

Source citation:

Yahoo! (2024, May 3). Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD). Yahoo! Finance. https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/BTC-USD/history/?guccounter=1&period1=1556841600&period2=1714767878

2.2: Determine whether the Bitcoin dataset connection is proper¶

It is important to verify that the BTC_USD dataset is connected to the Google Colab notebook before proceeding. A Linux command ls -l [filepath] conducts this verification.

In [19]:
! ls -l /content/drive/MyDrive/BTC_USD/

total 312
-rw------- 1 root root 159031 Mar  6 22:40 all_data.csv
-rw------- 1 root root  32296 Mar  6 22:40 test_data.csv
-rw------- 1 root root  94660 Mar  6 22:40 training_data.csv
-rw------- 1 root root  32158 Mar  6 22:40 validation_data.csv

2.3: Visualize the Bitcoin dataset¶

Tabular data for Bitcoin prices in the first week

As mentioned below, the BTC_USD.csv dataset spans over the previous 5 years of Bitcoin's existence. The table below illustrates the first week of this 5-year period (5/3/2019 to 5/9/2019):

In [20]:
alldata = pd.read_csv("/content/drive/MyDrive/BTC_USD/all_data.csv", names = ['Date', 'Open', 'High', 'Low', 'Close', 'Adj Close', 'Volume'])

alldata.iloc[1:8]
Out[20]:
Date Open High Low Close Adj Close Volume
1 2019-05-03 5505.552246 5865.881836 5490.201660 5768.289551 5768.289551 18720780006
2 2019-05-04 5769.202637 5886.893555 5645.469238 5831.167480 5831.167480 17567780766
3 2019-05-05 5831.068359 5833.862793 5708.035156 5795.708496 5795.708496 14808830723
4 2019-05-06 5791.693359 5802.957520 5653.687500 5746.807129 5746.807129 15737171804
5 2019-05-07 5745.599121 5988.178223 5741.395996 5829.501465 5829.501465 18026409033
6 2019-05-08 5849.481445 5989.980957 5794.715820 5982.457520 5982.457520 15320605300
7 2019-05-09 5982.316406 6183.039063 5982.316406 6174.528809 6174.528809 16784645411

Tabular data for Bitcoin prices in the previous week

This table illustrates the most recent week of Bitcoin's pricing. The time period ranges between 4/27/2024 to 5/3/2024.

In [21]:
alldata = pd.read_csv("/content/drive/MyDrive/BTC_USD/all_data.csv", names = ['Date', 'Open', 'High', 'Low', 'Close', 'Adj Close', 'Volume'])

alldata.tail(7)
Out[21]:
Date Open High Low Close Adj Close Volume
1822 2024-04-27 63750.988281 63898.363281 62424.718750 63419.140625 63419.140625 19530783039

1824 2024-04-29 63106.363281 64174.878906 61795.457031 63841.121094 63841.121094 26635912073
1825 2024-04-30 63839.417969 64703.332031 59120.066406 60636.855469 60636.855469 37840840057
1826 2024-05-01 60609.496094 60780.500000 56555.292969 58254.011719 58254.011719 48439780271

1828 2024-05-03 59087.492188 62671.398438 58857.058594 62671.398438 62671.398438 31852531712

2.4: Inspect and parse the data¶

In order to graphically visualize Bitcoin's closing price patternicity within the past 5 years, we must first inspect and parse the data. This parsing will allow a plot to generate.

In [22]:
data = "/content/drive/MyDrive/BTC_USD/all_data.csv"
with open(data) as f:
    data = f.read()

lines = data.split("\n")
header = lines[0].split(",")
lines = lines[1:]

crypto_price = np.zeros((len(lines),))
raw_data = np.zeros((len(lines), len(header) - 1))
for i, line in enumerate(lines):
    values = [float(x) for x in line.split(",")[1:]]
    crypto_price[i] = values[4]                               # Extracts the 6th column of the dataset (Adjusted Closing Price)
    raw_data[i, :] = values[:]

Plot for Bitcoin prices in the 5-year period

In the plot below, it appears that the plot contains three relatively short periods of prices approaching local maxima. In 2021 (at around 700 to 950 days after 5/3/19), prices reached approximately 64,000 dollars and 69,000 dollars respectively. An extremely high value of approximately 72,000 dollars occurred in 2024. Between those three periods of local maxima, cryptocurrency prices appeared to be less than half of these maximum values. For instance, the closing price in 2021 dropped from a local maximum of 64,000 dollars to a local minimum of just over 30,000 dollars.

In [23]:
plt.plot(range(len(crypto_price)), crypto_price)

plt.title("Bitcoin Prices (5/3/2019 - 5/3/2024)")
plt.xlabel("Days since 5/3/2019")
plt.xticks(range(0, len(crypto_price), 365))     #x -ticks every 365 days to represent a year from start date.
plt.ylabel("Adjusted Closing Price ($)")

plt.show()

The observation above is important to note because the extreme price fluctutations are characteristic of cryptocurrency's volatile nature. That being said, accurately predicting correct future prices proves to be a significant problem in Deep Learning. That is why it is important to start by overfitting the dataset, concoct a high-accuracy model, and improve on it by using augmentation and other Deep Learning techniques.

Step 3: Create an overfitting model¶

3.1: Normalize the data with MinMaxScaler¶

In [ ]:
scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_raw_data = scaler.fit_transform(raw_data)

target_scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_targets = target_scaler.fit_transform(crypto_price.reshape(-1, 1)).flatten()

3.2: Instantiate the dataset¶

In [ ]:
all_sample_count = int(len(raw_data))
print("Number of samples in entire dataset:", all_sample_count)

sampling_rate = 1
sequence_length = 7
batch_size = 64

train_dataset = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data,
    targets=scaled_targets,
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size)

Number of samples in entire dataset: 1828

3.3: Create the model¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.LSTM(6, input_shape=(sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(4, return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(2, activation="relu"),

    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/keras/src/layers/rnn/rnn.py:199: UserWarning: Do not pass an `input_shape`/`input_dim` argument to a layer. When using Sequential models, prefer using an `Input(shape)` object as the first layer in the model instead.
  super().__init__(**kwargs)
In [ ]:
model.summary()

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Layer (type)                    ┃ Output Shape           ┃       Param # ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ lstm (LSTM)                     │ (None, 7, 6)           │           312 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ lstm_1 (LSTM)                   │ (None, 7, 4)           │           176 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ lstm_2 (LSTM)                   │ (None, 2)              │            56 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense (Dense)                   │ (None, 1)              │             3 │
└─────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴───────────────┘

 Total params: 547 (2.14 KB)

 Trainable params: 547 (2.14 KB)

 Non-trainable params: 0 (0.00 B)

3.4: Compile and run the model¶

In [ ]:
model.compile(optimizer="adam", loss="mse", metrics=["mae", "mse", "RootMeanSquaredError"])
history = model.fit(train_dataset,
                    epochs=100)

Epoch 1/100

3.5: Graphically analyze the results/metrics¶

In [ ]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(12, 3))

axes[0].plot(history.history['loss'])
axes[0].set_title('Loss')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Loss')
axes[0].set_yscale('log')

axes[1].plot(history.history['mae'])
axes[1].set_title("Mean Absolute Error (MAE)")
axes[1].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
axes[1].set_ylabel('MAE')
axes[1].set_yscale('log')

axes[2].plot(history.history['mse'])
axes[2].set_title("Mean Squared Error (MSE)")
axes[2].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
axes[2].set_ylabel('MSE')
axes[2].set_yscale('log')

axes[3].plot(history.history['RootMeanSquaredError'])
axes[3].set_title("Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)")
axes[3].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
axes[3].set_ylabel('RMSE')
axes[3].set_yscale('log')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Step 4: Divide the data into training, validation, and test sets¶

For the next phase of Bitcoin price predictions, the model must create predictions while allowing the analysis of three different phases: training, validation, and testing. For this reason, The BTC_USD.csv data must be divided into three datasets that reflect the three phases. This step allows a more thorough and accurate analysis of the model's performance.

4.1: Calculate and display the number of samples¶

The entire BTC_USD.csv dataset contains 1,828 rows of data. The data was divided into three sets:

  1. Training set (60% of the entire set).
  1. Validation set (20% of the entire set).
  1. Test set (20% of the entire set).

The training set will represent seen data, whereas the validation and test sets will represent unnseen data. For this reason, the data rows in the csv file will all contain unique data in separate folders to prevent data leaking.

Also, each dataset contains data that is based on timeliness. This measure was taken to maximize the effectiveness of the model as it attempts to predict cryptocurrency prices. The breakdown of data is as follows:

1.) The training set contains Bitcoin pricing data between 5/3/2019 and 5/2/2022.

2.) The validation set contains data between 5/3/2022 and 5/2/2023.

3.) The test set contains data between 5/3/2023 and 5/3/2024.

In [ ]:
training_sample_count = int(0.6 * len(raw_data))
validation_sample_count = int(0.2 * len(raw_data))
test_sample_count = len(raw_data) - training_sample_count - validation_sample_count

all_sample_count = training_sample_count + validation_sample_count + test_sample_count

print("Number of samples in entire dataset:", all_sample_count)

print("\nNumber of training samples:", training_sample_count)
print("Number of validation samples:", validation_sample_count)
print("Number of test samples:", test_sample_count)

Number of samples in entire dataset: 1828

Number of training samples: 1096
Number of validation samples: 365
Number of test samples: 367

Visualizing the number of samples in each dataset

In [ ]:
data_type = ['Training', 'Validation', 'Test']
number_of_samples = [training_sample_count, validation_sample_count, test_sample_count]

plt.bar(data_type, number_of_samples)

plt.title('Splitting the Dataset for the Model')
plt.xlabel('Type of Dataset')
plt.ylabel('# of Samples')

for i in range(len(data_type)):
    plt.text(i, number_of_samples[i] + 1, str(number_of_samples[i]), ha='center')

4.2: Determine whether the Bitcoin training, validation, and test set connections are proper¶

For each dataset, the Linux command ls -l [filepath] is used once more to verify whether they are connected to the Google Colab notebook. This process must be successful before creating an optimal model.

In [25]:
! ls -l /content/drive/MyDrive/BTC_USD/

total 312
-rw------- 1 root root 159031 Mar  6 22:40 all_data.csv
-rw------- 1 root root  32296 Mar  6 22:40 test_data.csv
-rw------- 1 root root  94660 Mar  6 22:40 training_data.csv
-rw------- 1 root root  32158 Mar  6 22:40 validation_data.csv

4.3: Verify that each dataset works¶

The training set:

Step 5: Create an optimal model¶

5.1: Normalize the data with MinMaxScaler¶

In [ ]:
scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_raw_data = scaler.fit_transform(raw_data)

target_scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_targets = target_scaler.fit_transform(crypto_price.reshape(-1, 1)).flatten()

5.2: Instantiate the training, validation, and test datasets¶

In [ ]:
sampling_rate = 1
sequence_length = 7
delay = sampling_rate * (sequence_length + 24 - 1)
batch_size = 64

training_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=0,
    end_index=all_sample_count - delay - 1)

validation_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=all_sample_count - validation_sample_count - delay,
    end_index=all_sample_count - delay - 1)

test_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=training_sample_count + validation_sample_count)

print("\nNumber of training samples:", training_sample_count)
print("Number of validation samples:", validation_sample_count)
print("Number of test samples:", test_sample_count)

Number of training samples: 1096
Number of validation samples: 365
Number of test samples: 367

5.3: Visualize the shapes for samples and targets¶

In [ ]:
for samples, targets in training_set:
     print("samples shape:", samples.shape)
     print("targets shape:", targets.shape)
     break

samples shape: (64, 7, 6)
targets shape: (64,)

5.4: Determine a common baseline for MAE, MSE, and RMSE¶

In [ ]:
def evaluate_naive_method(dataset):
    total_abs_err = 0.
    total_squared_err = 0.
    samples_seen = 0
    for samples, targets in dataset:
        preds_normalized = samples[:, -1, 1]

        abs_err = np.abs(preds_normalized - targets)
        squared_err = (preds_normalized - targets) ** 2

        total_abs_err += np.sum(abs_err)
        total_squared_err += np.sum(squared_err)

        samples_seen += samples.shape[0]

    mae = total_abs_err / samples_seen
    mse = total_squared_err / samples_seen
    rmse = np.sqrt(mse)

    return mae, mse, rmse

val_mae, val_mse, val_rmse = evaluate_naive_method(validation_set)
test_mae, test_mse, test_rmse = evaluate_naive_method(test_set)

print(f"Validation MAE: {val_mae:.2f}")
print(f"Validation MSE: {val_mse:.2f}")
print(f"Validation RMSE: {val_rmse:.2f}")

print(f"\nTest MAE: {test_mae:.2f}")
print(f"Test MSE: {test_mse:.2f}")
print(f"Test RMSE: {test_rmse:.2f}")

Validation MAE: 0.06
Validation MSE: 0.01
Validation RMSE: 0.09

Test MAE: 0.06
Test MSE: 0.01
Test RMSE: 0.09

5.5: Create the model¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.LSTM(40, input_shape=(sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(20, return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(10, activation="relu"),

    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

callbacks = [
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint("bitcoin_lstm.keras",
                                    save_best_only=True)
]

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/keras/src/layers/rnn/rnn.py:199: UserWarning: Do not pass an `input_shape`/`input_dim` argument to a layer. When using Sequential models, prefer using an `Input(shape)` object as the first layer in the model instead.
  super().__init__(**kwargs)
In [ ]:
model.summary()

Model: "sequential_1"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Layer (type)                    ┃ Output Shape           ┃       Param # ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ lstm_3 (LSTM)                   │ (None, 7, 40)          │         7,520 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ lstm_4 (LSTM)                   │ (None, 7, 20)          │         4,880 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ lstm_5 (LSTM)                   │ (None, 10)             │         1,240 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense_1 (Dense)                 │ (None, 1)              │            11 │
└─────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴───────────────┘

5.6: Compile and run the model¶

In [ ]:
early_stopping = EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss',
                               patience=20,
                               restore_best_weights=True)

model.compile(optimizer="adamax", loss="mse", metrics=["mae", "mse", "RootMeanSquaredError"])

history = model.fit(training_set,
                    epochs= 200,
                    validation_data=validation_set,
                    callbacks=[callbacks, early_stopping])

model = keras.models.load_model("bitcoin_lstm.keras")

input_data = np.random.random((1, sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]))  # Adjust according to your sequence length

predicted_prices = []
predicted_changes = []

# This loop is responsible for making day-by-day cryptocurrency predictions.
for _ in range(7):
    next_day_change = model.predict(input_data)
    predicted_changes.append(next_day_change[0, 0])

    next_input_data = np.roll(input_data, -1, axis=1)
    next_input_data[0, -1] = next_day_change

    # Update the input data for the next prediction
    input_data = next_input_data

# Reshape the predicted changes array to match the shape of the original scaled data
predicted_changes_reshaped = np.array(predicted_changes).reshape(-1, 1)
predicted_changes_repeated = np.repeat(predicted_changes_reshaped, 6, axis=1)

predicted_prices = scaler.inverse_transform(predicted_changes_repeated)

for i, price in enumerate(predicted_prices, start=1):
    print(f"Day {i}: ${price[4]:.2f}")  # The Adjusted Closing Price is at index 4 (column 5 of table)

5.7: Graphically analyze the results/metrics¶

In [ ]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(12, 3))

axes[0].plot(history.history['loss'])
axes[0].set_title('Loss')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Loss')
axes[0].set_yscale('log')

axes[1].plot(history.history['mae'])
axes[1].set_title("Mean Absolute Error (MAE)")
axes[1].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
axes[1].set_ylabel('MAE')
axes[1].set_yscale('log')

axes[2].plot(history.history['mse'])
axes[2].set_title("Mean Squared Error (MSE)")
axes[2].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
axes[2].set_ylabel('MSE')
axes[2].set_yscale('log')

axes[3].plot(history.history['RootMeanSquaredError'])
axes[3].set_title("Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)")
axes[3].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
axes[3].set_ylabel('RMSE')
axes[3].set_yscale('log')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Step 6: Apply data augmentation to the model¶

6.1: Create and compile a model with Time Shifting:¶

a.) Create shifted data¶

In [ ]:
shift_range = range(-3, 4)  # Shift data by -3 to +3 time steps

def generate_time_shifted_data(data, targets, sequence_length, shift_range):
    shifted_data, shifted_targets = [], []
    for shift in shift_range:
        shifted_data.append(np.roll(data, shift, axis=0))
        shifted_targets.append(np.roll(targets, shift, axis=0))
    shifted_data = np.concatenate(shifted_data)
    shifted_targets = np.concatenate(shifted_targets)
    return shifted_data[:len(data)], shifted_targets[:len(targets)]

b.) Instantiate training, validation, and test datasets¶

In [ ]:
sampling_rate = 1
sequence_length = 7
shift_range = range(-3, 4)  # Shift data by -3 to +3 time steps
delay = sampling_rate * (sequence_length + 24 - 1)
batch_size = 64

# Training data
X_train_shifted, y_train_shifted = generate_time_shifted_data(scaled_raw_data[:-delay], scaled_targets[delay:], sequence_length, shift_range)

#  Validation data
X_val_shifted, y_val_shifted = generate_time_shifted_data(scaled_raw_data[:-delay], scaled_targets[delay:], sequence_length, shift_range)
val_start_index = all_sample_count - validation_sample_count - delay
X_val_shifted = X_val_shifted[val_start_index:]
y_val_shifted = y_val_shifted[val_start_index:]

# Test data
X_test_shifted, y_test_shifted = generate_time_shifted_data(scaled_raw_data[:-delay], scaled_targets[delay:], sequence_length, shift_range)
test_start_index = training_sample_count + validation_sample_count
X_test_shifted = X_test_shifted[test_start_index:]
y_test_shifted = y_test_shifted[test_start_index:]

training_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    X_train_shifted,
    targets=y_train_shifted,
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=0,
    end_index=len(X_train_shifted) - 1
)

validation_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    X_val_shifted,
    targets=y_val_shifted,
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=0,
    end_index=len(X_val_shifted) - 1
)

test_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    X_test_shifted,
    targets=y_test_shifted,
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=0,
    end_index=len(X_test_shifted) - 1
)

print("\nNumber of training samples:", len(X_train_shifted))
print("Number of validation samples:", len(X_val_shifted))
print("Number of test samples:", len(X_test_shifted))

Number of training samples: 1798
Number of validation samples: 365
Number of test samples: 337

c.) Construct the model¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.LSTM(40, input_shape=(sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(20, return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(10, activation="relu"),

    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

callbacks = [
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint("bitcoin_lstm.keras",
                                    save_best_only=True)]

model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.LSTM(40, input_shape=(sequence_length, scaled_raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(20, return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(10, activation="relu"),
    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/keras/src/layers/rnn/rnn.py:199: UserWarning: Do not pass an `input_shape`/`input_dim` argument to a layer. When using Sequential models, prefer using an `Input(shape)` object as the first layer in the model instead.
  super().__init__(**kwargs)

d.) Compile and run the model¶

In [ ]:
model.compile(optimizer="adamax", loss="mse", metrics=["mae", "mse", "RootMeanSquaredError"])

history = model.fit(training_set,
                    epochs= 200,
                    validation_data=validation_set,
                    callbacks=[callbacks, early_stopping])

model = keras.models.load_model("bitcoin_lstm.keras")

input_data = np.random.random((1, sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]))  # Adjust according to your sequence length

predicted_prices = []
predicted_changes = []

# This loop is responsible for making day-by-day cryptocurrency predictions.
for _ in range(7):
    next_day_change = model.predict(input_data)
    predicted_changes.append(next_day_change[0, 0])

    next_input_data = np.roll(input_data, -1, axis=1)
    next_input_data[0, -1] = next_day_change

    # Update the input data for the next prediction
    input_data = next_input_data

# Reshape the predicted changes array to match the shape of the original scaled data
predicted_changes_reshaped = np.array(predicted_changes).reshape(-1, 1)
predicted_changes_repeated = np.repeat(predicted_changes_reshaped, 6, axis=1)

predicted_prices = scaler.inverse_transform(predicted_changes_repeated)

for i, price in enumerate(predicted_prices, start=1):
    print(f"Day {i}: ${price[4]:.2f}")  # The Adjusted Closing Price is at index 4 (column 5 of table)

time_shifting_loss = history.history['loss']
time_shifting_mae = history.history['mae']
time_shifting_mse = history.history['mse']
time_shifting_rmse = history.history['RootMeanSquaredError']

Epoch 1/200

Day 1: $42268.69
Day 2: $39411.36

e.) Evaluate the model¶

In [ ]:
valid_loss, valid_MAE, valid_MSE, valid_RMSE = model.evaluate(validation_set, verbose=2)

6/6 - 2s - 370ms/step - RootMeanSquaredError: 0.0853 - loss: 0.0073 - mae: 0.0642 - mse: 0.0073

6.2: Create and compile a model with Noise Injection:¶

a.) Create normalized data with MinMaxScaler¶

In [ ]:
scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_raw_data = scaler.fit_transform(raw_data)

target_scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_targets = target_scaler.fit_transform(crypto_price.reshape(-1, 1)).flatten()

b.) Instantiate training, validation, and test datasets¶

In [ ]:
sampling_rate = 1
sequence_length = 7
shift_range = range(-3, 4)  # Shift data by -3 to +3 time steps
delay = sampling_rate * (sequence_length + 24 - 1)
batch_size = 64

scaled_raw_data = scaler.fit_transform(raw_data)
scaled_targets = tf.reshape(target_scaler.fit_transform(crypto_price.reshape(-1, 1)), -1)

# Function to add noise to data
def add_noise(data, noise_level=0.05):
    noise = np.random.normal(loc=0, scale=noise_level, size=data.shape)
    return data + noise

# Add noise to the scaled raw data
noisy_scaled_raw_data = add_noise(scaled_raw_data)

training_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=0,
    end_index=all_sample_count - delay - 1)

validation_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=all_sample_count - validation_sample_count - delay,
    end_index=all_sample_count - delay - 1)

test_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=training_sample_count + validation_sample_count)

print("\nNumber of training samples:", training_sample_count)
print("Number of validation samples:", validation_sample_count)
print("Number of test samples:", test_sample_count)

Number of training samples: 1096
Number of validation samples: 365
Number of test samples: 367

c.) Construct the model¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.LSTM(40, input_shape=(sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(20, return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(10, activation="relu"),

    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

callbacks = [
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint("bitcoin_lstm.keras",
                                    save_best_only=True)
]

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/keras/src/layers/rnn/rnn.py:199: UserWarning: Do not pass an `input_shape`/`input_dim` argument to a layer. When using Sequential models, prefer using an `Input(shape)` object as the first layer in the model instead.
  super().__init__(**kwargs)

d.) Compile and run the model¶

In [ ]:
early_stopping = EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss',
                               patience=20,
                               restore_best_weights=True)

model.compile(optimizer="adamax", loss="mse", metrics=["mae", "mse", "RootMeanSquaredError"])

history = model.fit(training_set,
                    epochs= 200,
                    validation_data=validation_set,
                    callbacks=[callbacks, early_stopping])

model = keras.models.load_model("bitcoin_lstm.keras")

input_data = np.random.random((1, sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]))  # Adjust according to your sequence length

predicted_prices = []
predicted_changes = []

# This loop is responsible for making day-by-day cryptocurrency predictions.
for _ in range(7):
    next_day_change = model.predict(input_data)
    predicted_changes.append(next_day_change[0, 0])

    next_input_data = np.roll(input_data, -1, axis=1)
    next_input_data[0, -1] = next_day_change

    # Update the input data for the next prediction
    input_data = next_input_data

# Reshape the predicted changes array to match the shape of the original scaled data
predicted_changes_reshaped = np.array(predicted_changes).reshape(-1, 1)
predicted_changes_repeated = np.repeat(predicted_changes_reshaped, 6, axis=1)

predicted_prices = scaler.inverse_transform(predicted_changes_repeated)

for i, price in enumerate(predicted_prices, start=1):
    print(f"Day {i}: ${price[4]:.2f}")  # The Adjusted Closing Price is at index 4 (column 5 of table)

noise_injection_loss = history.history['loss']
noise_injection_mae = history.history['mae']
noise_injection_mse = history.history['mse']
noise_injection_rmse = history.history['RootMeanSquaredError']

Epoch 1/200

Day 1: $37971.08

e.) Evaluate the model¶

In [ ]:
valid_loss, valid_MAE, valid_MSE, valid_RMSE = model.evaluate(validation_set, verbose=2)

6/6 - 3s - 520ms/step - RootMeanSquaredError: 0.0780 - loss: 0.0061 - mae: 0.0599 - mse: 0.0061

6.3: Create and compile a model with Time Warping¶

a.) Create normalized data with MinMaxScalar¶

In [ ]:
scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_raw_data = scaler.fit_transform(raw_data)

target_scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_targets = target_scaler.fit_transform(crypto_price.reshape(-1, 1)).flatten()

def time_warping(data, warp_factor):
    original_time = np.arange(len(data))
    warped_time = np.arange(0, len(data), warp_factor)

    interpolator = interp1d(original_time, data, kind='linear')
    warped_data = interpolator(warped_time)

    return warped_data

warp_factor = 1.5  # Adjust warp factor as needed
warped_scaled_raw_data = np.apply_along_axis(time_warping, axis=0, arr=scaled_raw_data, warp_factor=warp_factor)

b.) Instantiate training, validation, and test datasets¶

In [ ]:
sampling_rate = 1
sequence_length = 7
delay = sampling_rate * (sequence_length + 24 - 1)
batch_size = 64

training_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=0,
    end_index=all_sample_count - delay - 1)

validation_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=all_sample_count - validation_sample_count - delay,
    end_index=all_sample_count - delay - 1)

test_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=training_sample_count + validation_sample_count)

print("\nNumber of training samples:", training_sample_count)
print("Number of validation samples:", validation_sample_count)
print("Number of test samples:", test_sample_count)

Number of training samples: 1096
Number of validation samples: 365
Number of test samples: 367

c.) Construct the model¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.LSTM(40, input_shape=(sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(20, return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(10, activation="relu"),

    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

callbacks = [
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint("bitcoin_lstm.keras",
                                    save_best_only=True)
]

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/keras/src/layers/rnn/rnn.py:199: UserWarning: Do not pass an `input_shape`/`input_dim` argument to a layer. When using Sequential models, prefer using an `Input(shape)` object as the first layer in the model instead.
  super().__init__(**kwargs)

d.) Compile and run the model¶

In [ ]:
early_stopping = EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss',
                               patience=20,
                               restore_best_weights=True)

model.compile(optimizer="adamax", loss="mse", metrics=["mae", "mse", "RootMeanSquaredError"])

history = model.fit(training_set,
                    epochs= 200,
                    validation_data=validation_set,
                    callbacks=[callbacks, early_stopping])

model = keras.models.load_model("bitcoin_lstm.keras")

input_data = np.random.random((1, sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]))  # Adjust according to your sequence length

predicted_prices = []
predicted_changes = []

# This loop is responsible for making day-by-day cryptocurrency predictions.
for _ in range(7):
    next_day_change = model.predict(input_data)
    predicted_changes.append(next_day_change[0, 0])

    next_input_data = np.roll(input_data, -1, axis=1)
    next_input_data[0, -1] = next_day_change

    # Update the input data for the next prediction
    input_data = next_input_data

# Reshape the predicted changes array to match the shape of the original scaled data
predicted_changes_reshaped = np.array(predicted_changes).reshape(-1, 1)
predicted_changes_repeated = np.repeat(predicted_changes_reshaped, 6, axis=1)

predicted_prices = scaler.inverse_transform(predicted_changes_repeated)

for i, price in enumerate(predicted_prices, start=1):
    print(f"Day {i}: ${price[4]:.2f}")  # The Adjusted Closing Price is at index 4 (column 5 of table)

time_warping_loss = history.history['loss']
time_warping_mae = history.history['mae']
time_warping_mse = history.history['mse']
time_warping_rmse = history.history['RootMeanSquaredError']

Epoch 1/200

Day 1: $51884.14

e.) Evaluate the model¶

In [ ]:
valid_loss, valid_MAE, valid_MSE, valid_RMSE = model.evaluate(validation_set, verbose=2)

6/6 - 2s - 368ms/step - RootMeanSquaredError: 0.0795 - loss: 0.0063 - mae: 0.0614 - mse: 0.0063

6.4: Analyze the results¶

In [ ]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

augmentation_names = ["Time Shifting", "Noise Injection", "Time Warping"]
loss_values = [time_shifting_loss, noise_injection_loss, time_warping_loss]
mae_values = [time_shifting_mae, noise_injection_mae, time_warping_mae]
mse_values = [time_shifting_mse, noise_injection_mse, time_warping_mse]
rmse_values = [time_shifting_rmse, noise_injection_rmse, time_warping_rmse]

for i in range(3):
  print(f"\n\nGraphical results for {augmentation_names[i]}:\n")
  fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(12, 3))

  axes[0].plot(loss_values[i])
  axes[0].set_title('Loss')
  axes[0].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
  axes[0].set_ylabel('Loss')
  axes[0].set_yscale('log')

  axes[1].plot(mae_values[i])
  axes[1].set_title("Mean Absolute Error (MAE)")
  axes[1].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
  axes[1].set_ylabel('MAE')
  axes[1].set_yscale('log')

  axes[2].plot(mse_values[i])
  axes[2].set_title("Mean Squared Error (MSE)")
  axes[2].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
  axes[2].set_ylabel('MSE')
  axes[2].set_yscale('log')

  axes[3].plot(rmse_values[i])
  axes[3].set_title("Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)")
  axes[3].set_xlabel('Number of epochs')
  axes[3].set_ylabel('RMSE')
  axes[3].set_yscale('log')

  plt.tight_layout()
  plt.show()


Graphical results for Time Shifting:



Graphical results for Noise Injection:



Graphical results for Time Warping:

Step 7: Apply data regularization to the model¶

In Step 6, we looked at three different LSTM models--each with a unique augmentation method. Those augmentation methods were Time Shifting, Noise Injection, and Time Warping. Between those three models (and the original model from Step 5), the LSTM model with Noise Injection is the most optimal model for predicting cryptocurrency prices. This observation stems from the model's production of the lowest loss, MSE, and RMSE metrics. For this reason, we will use the LSTM model with Noise Injection in Step 7.

7.1: Normalize the data with MinMaxScaler¶

In [ ]:
scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_raw_data = scaler.fit_transform(raw_data)

target_scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_targets = target_scaler.fit_transform(crypto_price.reshape(-1, 1)).flatten()

7.2: Instantiate the training, validation, and test datasets¶

In [ ]:
sampling_rate = 1
sequence_length = 7
shift_range = range(-3, 4)  # Shift data by -3 to +3 time steps
delay = sampling_rate * (sequence_length + 24 - 1)
batch_size = 64

scaled_raw_data = scaler.fit_transform(raw_data)
scaled_targets = tf.reshape(target_scaler.fit_transform(crypto_price.reshape(-1, 1)), -1)

# Function to add noise to data
def add_noise(data, noise_level=0.05):
    noise = np.random.normal(loc=0, scale=noise_level, size=data.shape)
    return data + noise

# Add noise to the scaled raw data
noisy_scaled_raw_data = add_noise(scaled_raw_data)

training_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=0,
    end_index=all_sample_count - delay - 1)

validation_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=all_sample_count - validation_sample_count - delay,
    end_index=all_sample_count - delay - 1)

test_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=training_sample_count + validation_sample_count)

print("\nNumber of training samples:", training_sample_count)
print("Number of validation samples:", validation_sample_count)
print("Number of test samples:", test_sample_count)

Number of training samples: 1096
Number of validation samples: 365
Number of test samples: 367

7.3: Create and test one of the different models¶

In Step 7.3, we will work with models with three different regularization techniques: BatchNormalization, L1/L2 regularization, and Dropout. We will run only one of the models below before compilation (Step 7.4) and adjust regularization quantities to further improve the model. We will then analyze the results in Step 7.5 and 7.6.

7.3.1: Model with BatchNormalization¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.LSTM(40, input_shape=(sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu",),
    BatchNormalization(),
    layers.LSTM(20, return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    BatchNormalization(),
    layers.LSTM(10, activation="relu"),
    BatchNormalization(),
    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

callbacks = [
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint("bitcoin_lstm.keras",
                                    save_best_only=True)
]

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/keras/src/layers/rnn/rnn.py:199: UserWarning: Do not pass an `input_shape`/`input_dim` argument to a layer. When using Sequential models, prefer using an `Input(shape)` object as the first layer in the model instead.
  super().__init__(**kwargs)

7.3.2: Model with L1 and L2 Regularization¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.LSTM(40, input_shape=(sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu",
                kernel_regularizer=regularizers.l1_l2(l1=0.05, l2=0.05)),
    layers.LSTM(20, return_sequences=True, activation="relu", kernel_regularizer=regularizers.l1_l2(l1=0.05, l2=0.05)),
    layers.LSTM(10, activation="relu", kernel_regularizer=regularizers.l1_l2(l1=0.05, l2=0.05)),

    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

callbacks = [
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint("bitcoin_lstm.keras",
                                    save_best_only=True)
]

7.3.3: Model with Dropout¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.LSTM(40, input_shape=(sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    Dropout(0.5),
    layers.LSTM(20, return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    Dropout(0.5),
    layers.LSTM(10, activation="relu"),
    Dropout(0.5),

    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

callbacks = [
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint("bitcoin_lstm.keras",
                                    save_best_only=True)
]

7.4: Compile and run the model¶

In [ ]:
early_stopping = EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss',
                               patience=25,
                               restore_best_weights=True)

model.compile(optimizer="adamax", loss="mse", metrics=["mae", "mse", "RootMeanSquaredError"])

history = model.fit(training_set,
                    epochs= 200,
                    validation_data=validation_set,
                    callbacks=[callbacks, early_stopping])

model = keras.models.load_model("bitcoin_lstm.keras")

input_data = np.random.random((1, sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]))  # Adjust according to your sequence length

predicted_prices = []
predicted_changes = []

# This loop is responsible for making day-by-day cryptocurrency predictions.
for _ in range(7):
    next_day_change = model.predict(input_data)
    predicted_changes.append(next_day_change[0, 0])

    next_input_data = np.roll(input_data, -1, axis=1)
    next_input_data[0, -1] = next_day_change

    # Update the input data for the next prediction
    input_data = next_input_data

# Reshape the predicted changes array to match the shape of the original scaled data
predicted_changes_reshaped = np.array(predicted_changes).reshape(-1, 1)
predicted_changes_repeated = np.repeat(predicted_changes_reshaped, 6, axis=1)

predicted_prices = scaler.inverse_transform(predicted_changes_repeated)

for i, price in enumerate(predicted_prices, start=1):
    print(f"Day {i}: ${price[4]:.2f}")  # The Adjusted Closing Price is at index 4 (column 5 of table)

Epoch 1/200
28/28 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 14s 190ms/step - RootMeanSquaredError: 0.8287 - loss: 0.7830 - mae: 0.5175 - mse: 0.7830 - val_RootMeanSquaredError: 0.5467 - val_loss: 0.2988 - val_mae: 0.5051 - val_mse: 0.2988
Epoch 2/200

Day 1: $4970.79

7.5: Evaluate the model¶

In [ ]:
valid_loss, valid_MAE, valid_MSE, valid_RMSE = model.evaluate(validation_set, verbose=2)

6/6 - 2s - 390ms/step - RootMeanSquaredError: 0.0815 - loss: 0.0066 - mae: 0.0620 - mse: 0.0066

7.6: Analyze the results¶

In [ ]:
plt.plot(history.history['loss'], "bo")
plt.title('Loss')
plt.ylabel('Loss')
plt.yscale('log')

plt.xlabel('Number of epochs')
plt.legend(['Loss'], loc='upper right')
plt.show()
In [ ]:
plt.plot(history.history['mae'], "bo")
plt.title('Mean Absolute Error (MAE)')
plt.ylabel('MAE')
plt.yscale('log')
plt.yscale('log')
plt.xlabel('Number of epochs')
plt.legend(['Mean Absolute Error'], loc='upper right')
plt.show()
In [ ]:
plt.plot(history.history['mse'], "bo")
plt.title('Mean Squared Error (MSE)')
plt.ylabel('MSE')
plt.yscale('log')
plt.xlabel('Number of epochs')
plt.legend(['Mean Squared Error'], loc='upper right')
plt.show()
In [ ]:
plt.plot(history.history['RootMeanSquaredError'], "bo")
plt.title('Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)')
plt.ylabel('Root Mean Squared Error')
plt.yscale('log')
plt.xlabel('Number of epochs')
plt.legend(['RMSE'], loc='upper right')
plt.show()

Step 8: Try using pre-trained models and other architectures¶

8.1: Create and compile a Conv1D model

8.1: Normalize the data with MinMaxScaler¶

In [ ]:
scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_raw_data = scaler.fit_transform(raw_data)

target_scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
scaled_targets = target_scaler.fit_transform(crypto_price.reshape(-1, 1)).flatten()

8.2: Instantiate the training, validation, and test datasets¶

In [ ]:
sampling_rate = 1
sequence_length = 7
delay = sampling_rate * (sequence_length + 24 - 1)
batch_size = 64

training_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=0,
    end_index=all_sample_count - delay - 1)

validation_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=all_sample_count - validation_sample_count - delay,
    end_index=all_sample_count - delay - 1)

test_set = keras.utils.timeseries_dataset_from_array(
    scaled_raw_data[:-delay],
    targets=scaled_targets[delay:],
    sampling_rate=sampling_rate,
    sequence_length=sequence_length,
    shuffle=True,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    start_index=training_sample_count + validation_sample_count)

print("\nNumber of training samples:", training_sample_count)
print("Number of validation samples:", validation_sample_count)
print("Number of test samples:", test_sample_count)

Number of training samples: 1096
Number of validation samples: 365
Number of test samples: 367

8.3: Create and test one of the different models¶

In Step 8.3, we will work with four alternative models: a Conv1D model, a SimpleRNN, a GRU, and a Bidirectional RNN. We will run only one of the models below before compilation (Step 8.4) and analyze the results in Step 8.5 and 8.6.

8.3.1: A Conv1D model¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    Conv1D(10, 3, input_shape=(sequence_length, 6), activation="relu", strides=2),
    Conv1D(10, 3, activation="relu", padding='same'),
    layers.GlobalAveragePooling1D(),

    layers.Dense(32, activation="relu"),
    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

callbacks = [
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint("bitcoin_conv1d.keras",
                                    save_best_only=True)
]

8.3.2: A SimpleRNN model¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.LSTM(40, input_shape=(sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(20, return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    layers.LSTM(10, activation="relu"),

    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

callbacks = [
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint("bitcoin_lstm.keras",
                                    save_best_only=True)
]

8.3.3: A GRU model¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    GRU(40, input_shape=(sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    GRU(20, return_sequences=True, activation="relu"),
    GRU(10, activation="relu"),

    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

callbacks = [
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint("bitcoin_lstm.keras",
                                    save_best_only=True)
]

8.3.4: A Bidirectional LSTM model¶

In [ ]:
model = keras.Sequential([
    Bidirectional(layers.LSTM(40, input_shape=(sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]), return_sequences=True, activation="relu")),
    Bidirectional(layers.LSTM(20, return_sequences=True, activation="relu")),
    Bidirectional(layers.LSTM(10, activation="relu")),

    layers.Dense(1, activation="relu")
])

callbacks = [
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint("bitcoin_lstm.keras",
                                    save_best_only=True)
]

8.4: Compile and run the model¶

In [ ]:
early_stopping = EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss',
                               patience=20,
                               restore_best_weights=True)

model.compile(optimizer="adamax", loss="mse", metrics=["mae", "mse", "RootMeanSquaredError"])

history = model.fit(training_set,
                    epochs= 200,
                    validation_data=validation_set,
                    callbacks=[callbacks, early_stopping])

model = keras.models.load_model("bitcoin_lstm.keras")

input_data = np.random.random((1, sequence_length, raw_data.shape[-1]))  # Adjust according to your sequence length

predicted_prices = []
predicted_changes = []

# This loop is responsible for making day-by-day cryptocurrency predictions.
for _ in range(7):
    next_day_change = model.predict(input_data)
    predicted_changes.append(next_day_change[0, 0])

    next_input_data = np.roll(input_data, -1, axis=1)
    next_input_data[0, -1] = next_day_change

    # Update the input data for the next prediction
    input_data = next_input_data

# Reshape the predicted changes array to match the shape of the original scaled data
predicted_changes_reshaped = np.array(predicted_changes).reshape(-1, 1)
predicted_changes_repeated = np.repeat(predicted_changes_reshaped, 6, axis=1)

predicted_prices = scaler.inverse_transform(predicted_changes_repeated)

for i, price in enumerate(predicted_prices, start=1):
    print(f"Day {i}: ${price[4]:.2f}")  # The Adjusted Closing Price is at index 4 (column 5 of table)

Epoch 1/200

8.5: Evaluate the model¶

In [ ]:
valid_loss, valid_MAE, valid_MSE, valid_RMSE = model.evaluate(validation_set, verbose=2)

6/6 - 3s - 541ms/step - RootMeanSquaredError: 0.0815 - loss: 0.0066 - mae: 0.0620 - mse: 0.0066

8.6: Analyze the results¶

In [ ]:
plt.plot(history.history['loss'], "bo")
plt.title('Loss')
plt.ylabel('Loss')

plt.yscale('log')

plt.xlabel('Number of epochs')
plt.legend(['Loss'], loc='upper right')
plt.show()
In [ ]:
plt.plot(history.history['mae'], "bo")
plt.title('Mean Absolute Error (MAE)')
plt.ylabel('MAE')
plt.yscale('log')
plt.yscale('log')
plt.xlabel('Number of epochs')
plt.legend(['Mean Absolute Error'], loc='upper right')
plt.show()
In [ ]:
plt.plot(history.history['mse'], "bo")
plt.title('Mean Squared Error (MSE)')
plt.ylabel('MSE')
plt.yscale('log')
plt.xlabel('Number of epochs')
plt.legend(['Mean Squared Error'], loc='upper right')
plt.show()
In [ ]:
plt.plot(history.history['RootMeanSquaredError'], "bo")
plt.title('Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)')
plt.ylabel('Root Mean Squared Error')
plt.yscale('log')
plt.xlabel('Number of epochs')
plt.legend(['RMSE'], loc='upper right')
plt.show()
